So now that we've talked about how to use tonality, let's look at the other element of developing rapport, which is body language. And what this is about, the competency of having impact. How do you make a positive impression on someone, you know, right away in those first couple of seconds and build that massive conscious and unconscious rapport? Now one thing I will tell you is that nonverbal communication is literally ten times more powerful than verbal communication. Feeling, intention, thought, they're all communicated in the way that you move your body by the management of space, of time, your posture, your appearance, the gestures that you make, your facial expressions, the way you make eye contact with someone, even smell. All of that gets processed in a microsecond when you're speaking to someone and they're laying eyes on you for the first time. Now it's not so much that effective body language will close the deal for you. That's not what I'm saying here. What I'm saying is that ineffective body language will blow the deal for you. What it does, it stops you or simply doesn't allow you to get into rapport with someone else. They are repelled by what they see. Now as I said before, when a person lays eyes on you for the first time, bam, in that first one twenty-fourth of a second, that judgment indicator goes up and down. They see your face. They see how you move and they make a judgment. Essentially, they rip you apart. They kind of process you in their brain. They put you back together and you are judged. Now either you are judged as being a person that's sharp on the ball so they want to do business with or you're judged as someone they do not want to do business with. So when that simply repels them, someone they do not perceive as being an expert, someone they do not perceive as being sharp and enthusiastic, all the things that you need to bam get into that tight rapport. Now I want to tell you a little story here just to illustrate to you exactly how repulsive negative body language can be. It's a story about a seminar I gave in a ball place in Sydney, Australia, one of my favorite cities in the world. I had just gone through the whole section on body language in detail. Really delved into all the particular of eye contact, how you shake hands, how close you stand to somebody and that last issue of how close you stand. I was just literally on it for like 15 minutes calling people on stage and allowing builders to experience for themselves how awful it feels when someone really gets into your space bubble and invades it. So everyone's like, yeah, yeah, I get it. I can just see the audience gets it. You just know that everyone's right in tune. So I take a short break and as soon as I walk off the stage some wacky Australian that comes running up through with their thick axe and go, mate, mate, mate, he gets right in your face. I got this thing. I'm like, oh my God. And the guy invades my face like right there. And he's like, right to me. He's like, I got this thing. I got this thing. Mate, mate, mate, mate, mate. I'm like, oh my God, people want to kill myself. And I already had tuned out and somehow the guys like as I'm tuning out and like covering my, you know, trying to stop the spit, the guys tries to explain to me has this revolutionary invention called the express loo. I'm like, what's an express loo? It's a porta potty for five year olds. I'm like, a porta potty for five year olds. He wants to demonstrate to me how you actually sit on this little wooden porta potty, right? Anyway, long story short, I haven't cut to the point here. He ends up corralling not just me, but my manager who's Australian, my seminar promoter, every person he sees, he just runs up to them, gets right in their face. And every person walks there with that same exact feeling, which is, you know, I don't know about his product, but I would never do business with this guy in a million years. But the bottom line is this. Body language is not going to get you the sale, but the wrong body language will destroy the opportunity for you to make a sale.
